Name: The Invisible Man
Review:
The set design looks expensive. 
Elizabeth Moss looks expensive.
I was surprised twice during this movie, both times related to the main character's sister. It confused me to the point of bothering me that an unexplained email that she takes as gospel and performs absolutely zero follow-up is actually sufficient enough to dissolve their relationship entirely. When I realized her sister slammed the door in her face without question, I was surprised by the affect of the loneliness of the protagonist. The second surprise was when the sister died because that moment is fucking insane.

Name: 1, 2, 3, All Eyes On Me
Review:
I spent a lot of time thinking about this after I watched it the first time. The film deserves a content warning for the backdrop being a mass shooting of children. 

Initially, I rated this film horribly. I'm here now to defend it. I want to explain what I've come to understand about how I experienced it the first time.

The closest I've been to a school shooting was during my time at Mississippi State University. A student walking around on campus seeking help resulted in a miscommunication between school staff, leading to an alarm being rung out of the greatest sense of precaution. 

From their Facebook page, Starkville Police Department posted this message: "SPD responded to a reported Active Shooter on Mississippi State campus. One individual was taken into custody and no injuries or shots were reported. An all clear was issued and classes and normal campus operations will resume at 2:00 pm."

Looking at the police department's post from that day, there are comments where parents repeat false information their children gave them, as though they knew better than the police what had happened. There was not a real danger so it ultimately did not feel very real, because I was asleep at home and not on campus when the lockdown following the alarm transpired.

I had to listen to the stories of my friends before I had any idea what ripples of information did across the campus that day. The ones who did not know what was happening were reacting to text messages from the Maroon Alert system, learning incrementally that there was a reported active shooter. Though no shots were fired, I saw videos of frenzied students running from public view to find a place to hide and understood that fear had gripped my school in a new way. 

1, 2, 3, All Eyes on Me brings that clenched fear into a new perspective by focusing on the actions of one teacher, and one classroom. The film is not visually graphic, but the sound design is rooted in realism and will be extremely chilling.

The first time I watched it, it made me think of my dad. He is a second grade teacher. I watched it and I was angry because I did not want to think about my dad dying like that. He doesn't deserve it. No one does.

Mass gun violence is America's national shame. Recommending this film is difficult but it is necessary, because we need to be talking about why it happens, and how we can stop it from happening again.

I don't want anyone to ever feel the need to tell this story on film again.

Name: Enter the Void
Review:
It has been 22 days since I saw ENTER THE VOID and I am just now sitting down to record my thoughts.

This is my third Gaspar Noé film. 
The first one I watched was LOVE (2015). 
The second was CLIMAX (2018). 

Both of those entries helped prepare me for what I believe is Noé's most important story. The graphic nature of the sexual relationship explored in LOVE scandalized me, but it reveals Noé's own attitudes and revelations about sex as a creative force—inseparable from human nature. Dizzying heights of top-down camerawork in CLIMAX have their cinematographic roots in Noé's ENTER THE VOID. By the time I reached this film, I had a sense of the gravity that draws the filmmaker to such raw imagery. 

There isn't anything hidden in this film. The characters on their own are not redeemable but the trauma the viewer witnesses through the subjective lens of the protagonist is capable of invoking strong empathetic connections. I could not turn my face away, regardless of how great the feeling of dread grew in the space around me.

It is not possible for me to recommend this movie to anyone personally. I view this work similar to how I view ancient religious texts: there is wisdom contained within, but also much pain. It is an authorial vision capable of changing you for better or worse.

Name: The Green Knight
Review:
“Truth is like poetry. And most people fucking hate poetry.” ― Adam McKay
David Lowery approaches filmmaking as a poet and not a writer of prose. His films emphasize highly stylized visual language and emotional provocation over other things general audiences prioritize like action or romance. These things are present but they are put into their time. As someone who hated poetry in high school and has come to respect it as a medium, I see what David Lowery is doing. If I could say something to him, it would be "Thank you for sticking with this project and seeing it through to the end. I absolutely loved it."

Name: Eternals
Review:
I liked this movie.

It is a character movie and there are some interesting ones here. The characters somehow manage to overshadow the cosmic nature of Marvel's cosmic stories, but I mostly didn't mind. A consequence of this is that I also didn't really care about the "villains" as the Deviants were not very interesting to begin with, though this isn't a movie interested in clear cut heroes and villains.

Much of the humor didn't work for me and it tried way too hard. Other moments are lovingly elevated by Chloé Zhao's inspired hand.

One day, I hope I'm lucky enough to say, "See you tonight," to Kit Harrington.

Name: The Village
Review:
I’ve loved this movie since high school and I think it’s because I grew up walking in the woods. That’s where all my stories happened, and so I knew what that fear felt like on a visceral level. I camped on my family’s land alone even as a kid, so I say I got my Walden days out kind of early. Don’t get me wrong, I love nature and I love camping and I am intentional about giving myself that connection to nature on the regular. But ya boy likes sleeping in a bed. 

This is a good story because there are beautiful images of human emotions. Touch, color, and the choice of both is playfully displayed with rich meaning and love for the great mystery of life. 

Maybe I’m waxing poetic but this is a perfect movie for me.

Name: The Firm
Review: Scneider: "How long have you known the plaintiff?" Michael Scott: "I haven't actually seen it, but I have seen The Firm and I am planning on renting The Pelican Brief." Scneider: "How long have you known Ms. Levinson?" Scott: "Six years in 2 months." Scneider: "And you were you directly under her the entire time?" Scott: "That's what she said." Scneider: "Excuse me?" Scott: "That's what she said." Scneider: "Ms. Levinson told you she was your direct superior?" Scott: "Why would she say that?" Levinson: "Can we just move on?" Dunder-Mifflin counsel: "No, wait, I don't understand. Who's on record as saying this?" Scneider: "With all due respect I'm in the middle of a line of questioning. Now, Mr. Scott, what did you say Ms. Levinson said regarding your employment status with respect to her corporate position?" Scott: "Come again. That's what she said. I don't know what you're talking about." Levinson: "If I may, he was just telling a joke before. Could we move on to another question?" Scneider: "Are you sure?" Levinson: "Yes." Scneider: "Can you go back to where this digression began?" Clerk: "Mr. Scneider, 'and you were directly under her the entire time,' Mr. Scott, 'that's what she said.' " Scott: "Delivery's all wrong. Butchering it." Scneider: "Did Ms. Levinson ever say why she thought she was being fired?" Scott: "She thought it had to do with the twins. That's what I call them." Scneider: "Can you be more specific? Who are the twins?" Scott: "Uh, to be delicate... they hang off m'lady's chest. They... make milk." Scneider: "You don't need to go any further. Her breasts." Scott: "Yes." Scneider: "She thought she had something to do with her recent breast enhancement surgery." Scott: "Yes, and frankly the timing was nothing short of predominant." Scneider: "What about your romantic relationship with Ms. Levinson? Could that have played a part in her termination?" Scott: "Well if it did, then the company is breaking it's own rules." Scneider: "Interesting. How so?" Scott: "Because before we started dating we disclosed our relationship to HR and I have the proof right here." Dunder-Mifflin Counsel: "Okay. The company just has a few clarifying questions, Mr. Scott, if that's okay with you." Scott: "I'll allow it." DM Counsel: "Would you mind just taking a look at, uh, this photograph, please? That is you and Ms. Levinson in Jamaica, is that correct?" Scott: "Uh huh." DM Counsel: "And that photograph was taken two months prior to the start of your relationship, is that correct?" Scott doesn't respond. DM Counsel: "Mr. Scott, the timeline here is actually important please when did your relationship actually begin?" Scott: "Well, it depends on how you define begin. I mean, if it's from the first time we shook hands, like six years ago. If it's from the first time we kissed it's like two years ago." DM Counsel: "Excuse me?" Scott: "If it was the first time we kissed sober it was like four months after that." Scneider: "Can we take a short break?" DM Counsel: "No, are you telling me that your relationship began two years ago and not in February as you previously testified to here?" Scott: "Line." DM Counsel: "I'm sorry?" Executive: "He asked for a line. Like in a play."

Name: The Shining
Review: “See? It’s okay. He saw it on the television.” Mark incepted this into my brain and it is presently 25°F outside (in Mississippi) so it is requisite viewing. The following is a record of things I remember saying out loud or doing throughout the movie: - “Yikes. Yikes!” when the twins first appeared  - “Go away, go away!” when the twins turned their heads toward each other and laughed  - applaud (3 claps) when Dick telepathically asks what ice cream Doc wants  - eat milk & cereal when Dick and Doc eat the ice cream  - shift uncomfortably during Jack’s typewriting scenes (and pretty much anytime he’s on screen with Wendy)  - “Fuck y’all bitches,” when the twins invite Danny to play with them forever (& ever) (& pretty much every time they appear on screen) - “Don’t toy with me, Stanley,”  when he made this movie - rolled a joint when Jack goes to Room 237  - “All right bud,” when Dick’s artwork is on screen depicting bare-chested women - cuddle with cats  - “Ooh he used the hard R—forgot about that. Woof,” when the racist bathroom scene happened - smoke in anxious sympathy with Wendy when Danny does the redrum thing the first time - scratch knee anxiously at Jack and Wendy arguing with the baseball bat - clapped in rhythm with the strings during Danny's big redrum scene - literal heart attack during THE murder scene

Name: Pig
Review:
Watched with Ben

-----

Now that I've logged this movie 10 times in 2022 I think it's overdue to give Letterboxd some more words.

9 out of 10 of my screenings have been with company, i.e., I'm sharing this film with friends who heard me talk about it at the end of last year and said, "I'll watch that with you." It is a story about lonely characters and how they work with each other, so something about this sharing seems appropriate.

Nic Cage plays Rob, a former Portlander who lives in the woods. On a first viewing, it's easy to miss how sad he is at the start of the film. The entire arc of his character is bent on the grief he carries from the death of his wife. A few moments of quiet reflection gives us the image of a man who is broken, who knows he's broken, and who doesn't want to harm anyone else with his brokenness.

Alex Wolff plays an opportunistic seller of fine foods named Amir. He is meant to be more obnoxious, contrasting Robin's sullen and scraggly looks with bombastic and loud trappings of luxury and sophistication. Amir feels forced to help Rob when his pig goes missing but doesn't realize just how closely the two are tied.

It's not just these two characters who blew me away. The writing behind Nina Belforte's character Charlotte shows just how much attention Michael Sarnoski and Vanessa Block paid to making this story so real. Charlotte appears in only one scene in Part 3. Rob goes to visit her at the place where he used to run a restaurant and finds that it is now a bakery. Charlotte used to work for Rob, apparently, and she changed the restaurant a few years after he left. She waited for him to come back and take it, and when he didn't she said she realized it wasn't her. "I'm a baker," Charlotte explained matter-of-factly. Nearly the entirety of this scene is shot from a single angle, watching these two people with a lot of history from a corner. Something about the way she gingerly but confidently shows care for Rob gets me every time.

Anyway, I love this movie and think it's a great one to watch.

Name: The Night House
Review:
THE NIGHT HOUSE as a horror movie is really neat and has some very compelling visuals. I hit the spoiler button just so I could say this: HOT DAMN the scene in the bathroom with the TOUCHING felt so real! I could almost feel the tactile sensations.

The dynamics of this movie are incredibly well-tuned which is why I am extremely let down by the ending. It's not a story that gripped me enough for repeat viewings but if I ever see it again, I'll be curious to see how my thoughts change. 

Then again maybe the ending is supposed to be dissatisfying, fitting one of the themes of the film.

Name: All Light, Everywhere
Review: I loved part 3 and the epilogue. All Light, Everywhere is an essay on the interests of surveillance capitalism and the surveillance state at its core, with an addendum on filmmaking during the epilogue. Theo Anthony is quite transparent in ways I did not expect. Part 3 emotionally connected with me in a way that tells me this sequence was well-edited, yet it contains depictions of everyday vulnerabilities. There is a lot Anthony gives to consider here, and he gives it generously and with the best intentions. I'm not normally a documentary guy, and this doesn't really feel like a documentary (although it most definitely and quite tragically is)

Name: Irreversible
Review:
It took me 24 days to finally rate this film. 

The title is straightforward: watching this film is an irreversible event. Once it’s happened, it’s happened. You have lost some innocence and trust in humanity. Such is Gaspar Noé’s way. He tells stories in the most extreme way possible.

Be careful, this one is violent toward women and homophobic, though it is art and so it revels in the violence to the point of delight, begging the question of its creation and conception. 

May I never watch this film again, especially in my dreams.

Name: God Forbid: The Sex Scandal That Brought Down a Dynasty
Review:
From beginning to end, this documentary is filled with outrageous real-life characters who want nothing less than to rule the world. The facts presented by "The Pool Boy" for his story are shocking. 

Falwell is a cuck? Yeah, I remember those headlines.

Falwell started dating his wife when he was 18 and she was 13? Holy fucken shit man that floored me. 

All of this somehow being a catalyst leading to Jan. 6 and the rise of Christian Nationalism? Completely bonkers, and completely American.

The production of this doc added lots of spectacle: drone shots, dramatic reenactments of conversations, fast-paced music. It felt like someone wanted to make an Adam McKay doc and turned it up 120%

Name: Babylon
Review:
Babylon is an epic. It paints Hollywood to be the modern-day historical city-state along the Euphrates, a haven for science and knowledge.

The first hour is a film history lesson that happens to introduce the main characters. The second act is those characters’ reacting to the motion picture industry moving from silent films to sound pictures. 
The third act is how each character embraces the end of their careers. The final sequence is an epilogue that reads like a love letter to American cinema for the joys it has created in the years of its industrial toil, blood and water and cocaine and opium all. 

Themes of king making and king breaking create the throughline narrative. Chazelle’s own commentary is either obfuscated in the film’s pastiche references to many, many other films or plainly made in dialogue.

There is one scene I believe to be central to the thesis of the film: Jack’s confrontation of Elinor St John. This happens in the tail end of the second hour of the runtime, followed not long after by Jack’s suicide by 2h30m. 

I loved this film for all its messiness and beauty. There’s a good chance I see it for a third time in theaters. Your first should absolutely be on the big screen.

Name: The Whale
Review: this movie made me think about my own life  I am writing presently at 9:30 in the evening on Thursday, the 19th of January. I don't usually write longform reviews on Letterboxd because I prefer to write where the writing lives when it comes to digital media and I generally don't want to write things that live very long about the movies I watch except facts I find most important or interesting. Usually, my Letterboxd review for a movie includes the person or group with whom I'm sharing the experience. Sometimes, it's something else. I don't write unless I find it a thought worth writing, because I want to understand my thoughts. This film is about writing just as much as it is about dying. Leaving a legacy is one of the topic sentences reinforced by the narrative. Writing well takes time, it takes attention, and it takes the need to say something. People will say anything to avoid feeling uncomfortable, without realizing how uncomfortable they are themselves. People get angry about things they don't fully understand and project their own feelings onto others. We make excuses for ourselves. We're enabling habits we don't like to support people we do. I rated this film 2.5 out of 5. This is a low rating. It's not because I didn't like it. I watched this twice and brought more people the second time. I cried both times, but in truth the only reason I cried was because of Brendan Fraser, the symbolism of his own life, and what this movie made me think about my own life. My complaints are primarily with how manipulative the film feels. It is excellently edited and masterfully performed, but the ham-fisted approach toward Charlie's eating disorder feels cheap, and only subconsciously raised as an issue at all. Though Hong Chau plays a very nuanced character who both enables Charlie's bad habits and cares for him despite the consequences, she cannot have helped what was written for her in the playbook. I may have to read the book to validate that this complaint of mine is about the author, distracting from his own sad life. If so, I completely understand... it's just not something I can say is a great movie. And yet, this movie made me think about my own life

Name: Taxi Driver
Review: I see the comparisons people have made about First Reformed given Paul Schrader's hand in writing both and now understand better what others have said (how Ethan Hawke plays in a movie that's basically Taxi Driver but about climate change). But I'd rather contrast the two films, especially since they have different directors and ultimately, I believe, different theses that somehow arrive at the same conclusion. I know this won't be enough for me to say about this film and I'm going to guess I'll rewatch it soon, as I've yet to write about First Reformed at length and this seems like the right opportunity. Taxi Driver reminds me a lot of First Reformed but only because I was looking for that. I found in Robert de Niro a distinct character who is most definitely not Ernst Toller. The women who influence Travis Bickle's mad mind are parallel to the women who influence Toller, but other than the obvious narrative similarities they are not the same characters.

Name: We're All Going to the World's Fair
Review: please don't do it. just keep making videos. I wasn't surprised that David Lowery was an executive producer. This film is a visual poem, and visual poetry is Lowery's shit. It sets a long first verse and follows with a number of stanzas and a surprisingly emotional coda. Personally, I didn't think I would connect with this film emotionally after the first half hour made me feel nothing. But I like to think I'm a patient man and so I waited. I watched. There was so much that happened before I ever felt anything and then I was kind of blown away that I was on the verge of weeping by the end. It's an interesting narrative. The score and visuals all fixate on Anna Cobb and her character Casey to marvelous effect, and when the camera isn't looking at Casey it fills in the gaps of the imagination with other, specifically chosen examples. The directorial voice is present but not overpowering. I look forward to more from Jane Schoenbrun. One of my favorite mental activities during a movie is cataloguing "what am I thinking about right now." Another is "what does the director hope I'm thinking about right now." If the two don't seem to align at all, I have to wonder if this was a message intended for me. With this film, I can be certain that it most definitely was intended for me. I want to go to the world's fair.

Name: Air
Review:
I was able to watch this at a special screening hosted by the historic Capri Theatre in Jackson, MS

Honestly this movie does great when it’s focused on the conversations that have to take place to move the plot forward. The director found a real sweet spot with balancing the visual around the dialogue and it helped make every turn feel like it was really happening in front of me despite already knowing the outcome. Matt Damon is great but a standout performance from Jason Bateman surprised me. 

Soundtrack is great and the comedy was well-crafted for the audience that would be interested in this story, yet it still manages to be relevant to anyone who isn’t a sports junkie. 

It’s a good time. Just do it.

Name: Oppenheimer
Review:
1. Fission 
Cillian Murphy reaches back in time and across many years to summon a specter of a man. Oppenheimer is a complicated individual, insomuch that his politics is as paradoxical as the quantum world he is haunted by in visions. He is against the bomb but he built it. He didn’t build it but he designed it. He thought it might end the world but he didn’t stop the button being pushed. 

His personal relationships aren’t spared the consequences of a complicated man. His affairs reveal a man whose appetite is human, who eats and bleeds and breathes and drinks and fucks. Jean Tatlock presents the paradoxical woman, one who says she doesn’t want him but continues to call on him to satisfy herself. His marriage to Kitty by juxtaposition reflects a mature relationship of mutual respect and friendship. They “have walked through fire” and somehow survived the journey to the other side. His loyalties to his family and friends and country are put to the test and found to be strong, though sometimes in conflict with his recorded actions. Here is a man rich in the materials of life, and he continues to feel the weight of the quantum world he has helped unleash. 

Even his relationship to himself is troubled by the guilt he feels for his role in history. He admits he is not a good person in the same moment he is evidently not a good father. Despite his awareness of being selfish and awful he has a good and loyal friend to remind him that such people would not find that awareness.

The color of the man is shades of gray.

2. Fusion 
Robert Downey Jr’s Admiral Strauss is a man who believes himself a storied asset to his country. Strauss appears as the natural opponent of Oppenheimer, though he projects his own manipulation and calculated politics onto a man he does not fully understand. 

Nolan’s blending of multiple narratives that involve a wide range of characters is expertly executed. This film is visually, aurally, and emotionally mature. This film is his best work yet.

Name: Oppenheimer
Review:
Watched with Jack and Michael

RDJ’s performance stands out this time, especially that meeting before advising Truman about the H-bomb 

Something I’ve thought for a long time but haven’t written down: Chris Nolan doesn’t know how to write women so he just tends not to. There’s always at least one, but there’s never a movie that passes that arbitrary Bechdel test. He finds women he hopes to portray as smart but he cannot help reveal his underlying expectations of women when one considers the narrative importance Nolan allows for them. They get moments which are elevated by the actresses who Nolan is smart enough to hire for their professionalism and talent; I am recalling Emily Blunt, Florence Pugh, and Olivia Thrilby from this film, as each actress works hard to be a real person in sometimes less than ideally written roles. 

Emily Blunt’s performance stands out this time, especially that interrogation scene

Name: Call Me by Your Name
Review: is it better to speak or die? when i first watched this in the spring of 2017, it was the first movie featuring queer characters i'd seen on the silver screen. i was at the beginning of my career as a software engineer and budding into my own sexuality after college. i knew a bit of the background of the film from Timmee's interview with Ellen (I don't watch Ellen but somehow I watched this interview) and I loved what he said about this role: he talked about how lovely it was to work on this film and that since it was his first leading role he just wanted to get started. "I have the tremendous gift of absolutely no one knowing who I am, so I thought if this doesn't work then --" and he laughs, and Ellen laughs, and the audience laughs, " -- on to the rest of life!" Chalamet is a great actor with so much charm, and I suspect he's a wonderful person. his performance as Elio helped me visualize and understand my own journey in relationships with others. the first heartbreak is the one you remember for your whole life.

Name: Infraction
Review:
I found this short to be thoughtful and boldly made.

Through the collaborative medium of film, the filmmakers invite you into a story that acknowledges the consequences of racism in the U.S. justice system. Terrance Lewis is an inspiration, and I plan to donate funds to the Liberation Foundation to support their work to free wrongfully incarcerated human beings.

There's only one real action scene and it's over in the opening seconds. This story has no hero, only a metaphorical villain presented in the character of the warden. Everyone in prison is subject to constant surveillance and the system protects itself by requiring dehumanization at a policy level. The rest of the characters are humans caught in a bureaucratic system intent on maintaining power. Terrance Lewis is, indeed, a gift to society. 

Abolish prison and reform the criminal justice system. Automatic expungements for all wrongful incarcerations now!

Name: Dune
Review: If I see Part 2 in theaters again, it will tie with Oppenheimer at 9 in-theater viewings. I might see it a 10th time just to make it break the record. Part 1 is made better by Part 2. Initially, I thought shield combat was abandoned entirely in Part 2, but instead it was just visually "turned down," like volume when the music is too loud. It is still present, but it is less visually significant. Perhaps this was absolutely intentional (which would mean it's narratively significant) given that Part 2 upscales Arrakis as well as the entire plot. The multiple birthright seed planted during the initial scene for Gaius Helen Mohiam as well as her specific invocation of the phrase Kwisatz Haderach all set the stage for Paul's mental state as he descends on Arrakis and ultimately seeks to ascend the empire... it blooms so well in Part 2 that I burst with joy and terror. The Kwisatz Haderach is an impressive power, to be sure. But it all boils down to worms and holes, which is pretty wild when you think about the universe. "They see what they've been told to see."

Name: Aftersun
Review:
When I was eleven, no one remembered it was my birthday.
When I was ten-turning-eleven, I invited my entire homeroom to a birthday party at my house. None of them showed up. One of my dad's coworkers (another teacher at his school) brought her sons -- some boys a few grades younger than me who I didn't really know but generally liked -- and when they arrived and saw the multiple tables set up in the backyard and not another child in sight, turned to their mom and said, "Please don't leave us here." Watching this the first time, I knew exactly what this scene meant to Calum. I understood why he didn't want the camcorder on. It was a painful memory, and he was trying to create happy memories for his daughter.

Name: Civil War
Review: what is this film trying to say? it valorizes war photojournalism and adds some commentary to the ethics of the practice, but what does the film want to say about it all? as a filmmaking exercise, absolutely fantastic craft. but since Alex Garland has announced he's not interested in being behind the camera anymore, I join him and look forward to seeing his full focus in the writing process again. he's an excellent writer, and this film is written to put horrible things on display from beginning to end. it is anti-war, and that seems to be about the only thing it intends on being. except perhaps an invitation to an audience that paid for tickets to see this to reflect on why the images are on the screen, and have you considered who captured them so they could be there? i'm not rating this review, but there's a chance i go see this again just to be sure there aren't ideas i'm missing out on or things i wasn't able to pay attention to.

Name: Civil War
Review:
what is this film trying to say? it's not trying to comment on America's current politics. it's literally just a polemic on empathy and wartime journalism.

Jessie's question to Lee about taking her photo if she were to get shot is so instructive. 

i've settled on a rating and it's pretty positive. i was compelled to see this again within 24 hours and it's a movie speaking directly to americans. at the same time, it speaks to armed conflict in general.

there is no reason in war, and garland knew better than to try to show anything appealing to reason. "We record so that people ask," and the filmmaker hopes you ask.

bravo, alex garland. may your directing filmography be venerated.

Name: My Old Ass
Review:
I won't rate this review because I don't normally log a movie I didn't watch from start to finish. This experience was different and worth journalizing.

The trailer for this film appeared in the last month or so, and as a frequent theatergoer, I am familiar. Despite having Aubrey Plaza, I was never interested and considered myself outside of the intended audience. However, this was the film screened for my first time attending the Secret Movie Series.

Theater audiences are varied, but I understood the vibe of the people in this room quickly. No one was interested in this film. It was all couples just talking to each other, loudly crackling their plastic food containers and barely pretending to acknowledge other people exist.

I gave the film 20 minutes but decided I did not care about the $5 ticket, nor the waste of time driving to the theater, but I wouldn't subject myself to an experience I already was not enjoying. 

IDK what this movie is going to say, but I know I'll need to wait another time to try and listen.

Name: Megalopolis
Review: FFC sold his vineyards for this so i went to see it again since i fell asleep the first time (not an indictment of the movie). i don’t think i understand it completely but i love the big ideas i love how Kylo Ren and Missandei get together, halfway fulfilling a joke from the last season of Parks & Rec. i love how Shia Labeouf is a little shit gremlin who is petty and chaotic. i love how Aubrey Plaza is still pretty much April Ludgate, but even sexier. The excess is so extravagant. The narrative is so meandering. But it is hopeful, and an invitation to explore ideas. I imagine that folks with little patience will not enjoy this since it requires you to interpret it. And yet it is still very unsubtle with its subtext. this also has made me ask myself: WHAT THE FUCK IS A MOVIE?

Name: Ready or Not
Review: "And then I met Grace..." Ready or Not asks you to invest in its themes. This is truly a movie-lover's movie, as far as I'm concerned. This is an absolute treat in regard to story, but it delivers on so much more. As an exploration in the white wedding culture that dominates southern culture in the same industry, this film expresses a horror that very few have openly vocalized in public. Weddings in the Deep South ask too much of the bride, and Samara Weaving embodies the dark nature of this ritual with some necessary revenge. What a delightful treasure trove of tradition and treachery!

Review: i'll be honest... i loved this. i'm not a bob dylan fan, but that's not to say i'm not NOT a fan -- just unfamiliar. as a fan of movies, i really enjoyed getting to know bob dylan the person through his songs as performed by bob dylan the character. i recognized some and heard some new ones. the main thing i learned was that bobby didn't like to repeat himself, which i find infinitely relatable. as far as characters go, i really enjoyed chalamet's performance. Monica Barbaro was a fun match up. i'm grateful for some other familiar faces in the support cast like Scoot McNairy and Edward Norton and Elle Fanning. the fact that this film is light on the facts and leans more into how Bobby lives and loves worked for me. it's no Dune pt 2 but it's a lovely cap to this year

Name: Munchausen
Review:
By nursing secret destructive attitudes, a wife can drive her husband, and a mother her children, into illness, accident, or even death. Or she may decide to keep the children from marrying—a deeply hidden form of evil that rarely comes to the surface of the mother's conscious mind. (A naïve old woman once said to me, while showing me a picture of her son, who was drowned when he was 27: "I prefer it this way; it's better than giving him away to another woman.")
A strange passivity and paralysis of all feeling, or a deep insecurity that can lead almost to a sense of nullity, may sometimes be the result of an unconscious animus opinion. In the depths of the woman's being, the animus whispers: "You are hopeless. What's the use of trying? There is no point in doing anything. Life will never change for the better."
The process of individuation: Animus, the man within  M-L von Franz, excerpt from Man and His Symbols

Review:
a lot of fun but also a mess, narratively. 
feels like an amalgam of Bong Joon Ho tropes.

- I love Robert Pattinson’s acting 
- Title card at 35 minutes in the runtime is so much fun
- The title (and in general, just having watched a lot of movies) tells you everything (I mean, the plot is not subtle at all)
- The evil characters are comedic and just present enough on screen to deserve their foils; Mark Ruffalo seems to be having a real good time, and Toni Collette manages to terrify as well as tickle
- Naomi Ackie!!! she played self-possessed, protective girlfriend so well
- The creepers are kinda cute, and I love this whole talking animals thing we have going on (I wonder if Bong Joon Ho plays a Bard, or maybe a Druid)

this is my first criterion film to own, and i’ve expanded my collection to 28 titles and i’ve logged over a thousand more films to Letterboxd.

i love movies, and i think they can achieve high art. i’m grateful for all the experience offered by the film medium, i believe it has made me more human.

Name: The Brutalist
Review:
OVERTURE Chains and screams and joy and hugs as the Statue of Liberty enters the frame unexpectedly from the top, staring down at us like a giant mother. THE ENIGMA OF ARRIVAL Attila welcomes his cousin with tears and introduces him to his quarters as a servant, mentioning only the employee restroom. Attila's insecurity drives László out. The weakness of the man and the woman's suggestion create a reality that is actively hostile toward László. And this is how America seems to be designed: to separate, to subjugate, and to sell. Bicycle. Tricycle. Family. The act of immigration is an act of faith that life can be made, that we can choose better things for ourselves and give more to our friends and family. The promise of America is that all of this is possible. That promise will seem like a lie by the end of this film, but the hope in this story is not rooted in the American dream.
"Is there a better description of a cube than that of its construction?"
Doylestown represents small-town America, contrasting the NYC arrival with a rural community. The project is only welcomed by the community once it is demonstrated to be sufficiently Christian—a thread that is important in history because of the bloodshed it has caused. INTERMISSION I am pleased that the intermission exists in streaming, even if only for a minute. I did enjoy the music during the theatrical intermission. THE HARD CORE OF BEAUTY Only Maggie cares enough to show up with László to greet Zsófia and Erzsébet. I think this film shows women honestly, and we are meant to draw conclusions from how loud the men end up being. Perhaps my main criticism of this film is that we spend so much time with these men when I prefer the women, yet it reflects reality to spend more time listening to flawed men. Beauty's core is hard because it comes from truth. EPILOGUE Truth told through storytelling is an expression of the storyteller. The storyteller all along is Zsófia, and she succumbs to the same ill fate as her uncle László and survives her aunt Erzsébet to speak for him as the concluding point of this film. Harry rapes Zsófia, but it happens off-screen. Harrison rapes László. The Brutalist depicts this American story in America, but the rape depicted on screen happens in Italy. What are we to make of the rape that we know happened off-screen, in America—in our own Pennsylvania?

This was my seventh viewing, first one outside of an official movie theater. ICYDK: Clarksdale, MS, does not have a movie theater! Name: The Wicker Man
Review:
watched the extended edition with Morgan

the theatrical cut is indeed better, but here's a catalog of what is included in the extended edition (from my memory):
- more of Sgt Howie being a good Christian man on the mainland
- a too-early appearance by Lord Summerisle to present a young man for Willow
- an additional sequence of a song that was still included in the theatrical cut

this was a fun spooky season watch. Morgan cooked a venison stew with Welsh rarebit. I had Boddington's pub ale for libation.

Name: Weapons
Review: on a rewatch i'm considering the critical reviews and still finding so much to appreciate with this film some of the character work is a chore but it is perhaps a choice of style over substance; specifically, it's always been my perspective that Justine was written to be annoying but now i'm wondering if she is annoying because the person who was writing her is annoying and I don't know how to determine the difference and whether that even matters it still made me laugh out loud, it still captured my attention, it still fucking slaps also put down your phones! put them in another room when you watch a movie and actually let yourself be bored while you watch something for once. i promise, you will survive

Name: Midsommar
Review: i think why this captivated me so wholly after my first viewing is because this is Ari Aster channeling a folk storyteller and it's my favorite version of Aster's writing. his directing is excellent, though I admit he must be forced to cut-cut-cut in editing to tighten things up into a cohesive narrative. the director's cut extends some scenes, but the new scenes were entirely justified in being removed from the final cut. the theatrical cut is so effective at its 2h23m runtime -- this is the sweet spot. and the ending shocked me almost as much as the reactions I witnessed in the theater after the movie that first, fateful watch. i think i've talked about this on the jxn film club podcast before. anyway, the national folk festival is coming to jackson. i doubt there will be much folk horror there, but you can bet i'll be trying to talk about it (if it comes up organically); if not regarding Midsommar, it will be very easy for me to talk about Sinners -- which is definitely a Mississippi folk horror story.

Review: when Perfidia says, "I'm not okay" — 😢 i have been reading the source material, Thomas Pynchon's Vineland, and I'm on the final chapter. It's about 50 pages long, some other chapters have been like that. it's been a wild ride, and though this film is a loose adaptation by plot, it is pretty spot-fucking-on in terms of theme and character development. i'm curious to see how the ending of the novel stacks up to the ending of the film. i understand they're not at all similar. i'll probably watch this again whenever i can find someone to show it to.

Name: Wicked: For Good
Review: why didn't anyone tell me these films are about fascism? Glinda's entire arc is just: stay silent to stay safe → benefit from the system → realize you're complicit. Glinda embodies "well-meaning liberal who enables fascism" so well. Ariana Grande deserves everything. really surprised that i enjoyed these movies, though maybe i shouldn't be since i loved The Wizard of Oz growing up. in fact, my 3.5 star rating is for the musical performances, which stand on their own pretty well. the visual language of this film is so... bored. it's not interested in saying anything and the camera just stands like there a passive observer. i knew there was reason to be worried after they sucked all the color out of the set with whatever grading they did — almost like they were trying to broadcast they were sucking the life out of this adaptation. despite that, the story is told.

Name: Groundhog Day
Review:
[recognizes Phil and runs up to him on the street]
Ned: Phil? Hey, Phil? Phil! Phil Connors? Phil Connors, I thought that was you!
[thinking Ned's just a watcher of the local news]
Phil: Hi, how you doing? Thanks for watching.
[Starts to walk away]

Ned: Hey, hey! Now, don't you tell me you don't remember me because I sure as heckfire remember you.
[bemused]
Phil: Not a chance.
Ned: Ned...
[takes off his hat revealing his completely bald head]

Ned: Ryerson. "Needlenose Ned"? "Ned the Head"? C'mon, buddy. Case Western High. Ned Ryerson: I did the whistling belly-button trick at the high school talent show? Bing! Ned Ryerson: got the shingles real bad senior year, almost didn't graduate? Bing, again. Ned Ryerson: I dated your sister Mary Pat a couple times until you told me not to anymore? Well?
[not even a smidgen of recognition]
Phil: Ned Ryerson?
Ned: Bing!
Phil: Bing. So did you turn pro with that bellybutton thing, Ned?
Ned: No, Phil, I sell insurance.
[deadpan]
Phil: What a shock.
Ned: Do you have life insurance? Because if you do you could always use a little more. Am I right or am I right or am I right? Right? Right?
[feigning politeness and failing]
Phil: Ned, I'd love to stand here and talk with you... but I'm not going to. See you.
[laughs]
Ned: That's okay! I'll walk with you!
[does so, to Phil's annoyance]

Ned: See, whenever I see an opportunity, I charge it like a bull. "Ned the Bull", that's me now. You know, I have friends who live and die by the actuarial tables and I say, "Hey! It's all one big crap-shoot anywho!" Tell me, have you ever heard of single premium life because I think that could really be the ticket for you.
[stops Phil in admiration]

Ned: Oh, God! It is so good to see you! Tell me, what are you doing for dinner?
Phil: Something else. It's been great seeing you, "Needlehead". Take care.
[Phil steps off the curb into a deep, watery puddle. Ned laughs obnoxiously]

Ned: Whoa-ho-ho! Watch out for that first step! It's a doozy!
[as Ned continues to cackle, Phil stomps away]
